coredata.dc:description: Lithium-ion batteries have become the backbone of electrical vehicles, and managing their thermal behavior under high charge–discharge rates is increasingly important. This study introduces a novel spray-based immersion cooling method and experimentally compares its three partial submersion configurations against a conventional fully submerged flow-based immersion cooling system, using a 9S7P battery pack. The battery is operated under demanding charge and discharge rates ranging from 1C to 4C, while coolant flow rates are varied between 2 and 4 L/min. The results indicate that spray-based immersion cooling with minimal submersion achieves the lowest maximum cell temperatures and smallest temperature difference across the pack. This configuration reduces the dielectric liquid usage by up to 85 % compared to the flow-based approach. When subjected to high flow rates, this configuration delivers up to 19 % reduction in peak temperature, limiting it around 34 °C at 4C discharge rate. These results show that spray-based immersion cooling with low liquid volume and precise nozzle placement offers a highly promising direction for efficient, lightweight, and scalable battery thermal management system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Battery pack configuration showing thermocouple placement across the battery pack. Green markers indicate monitored cells.

Layout of the 9S7P battery pack within its housing showing the arrangements of thermocouples and the positions of spray nozzles. Green markers indicate monitored cells. Rows are designated as Left (L), Middle (M), and Right (R), while positions within a cell are denoted as Top (T), Center (C), and Bottom (B).

Schematic of the experimental setup for immersion cooling configurations. The layout shown corresponds to the spray immersion cooling (SIC) case, where the inlet is located at the top of the left-side wall of the housing and diverges into six spray nozzles positioned above the battery pack. For the flow immersion cooling (FIC) case, the inlet is located at the center of the left-side wall of the housing, allowing dielectric liquid to enter directly into the housing.
Schematic representation of the four cooling cases investigated.
Comparison of temperature profiles for cells M1, M5, and M9 during 4C charging at a flow rate of 4 L/min under (a) case I and (b) case IV.
Comparison of temperature profiles for cells M1, M5, and M9 during 4C discharging at a flow rate of 4 L/min under (a) case I and (b) case IV.
Maximum temperature comparison during 4C charging for all four cooling configurations at varying flow rates of (a) 2 L/min, (b) 3 L/min, and (c) 4 L/min.
Maximum temperature comparison during 4C discharging for all four cooling configurations at varying flow rates of (a) 2 L/min, (b) 3 L/min, and (c) 4 L/min.
Current and voltage curves during 4C (a) charging and (b) discharging.
Hottest and coldest regions in FIC and SIC.
Maximum temperature difference between cells during 4C charging for all four cooling configurations at varying flow rates of (a) 2 L/min, (b) 3 L/min, and (c) 4 L/min.
Maximum temperature difference between cells during 4C discharging for all four cooling configurations at varying flow rates of (a) 2 L/min, (b) 3 L/min, and (c) 4 L/min.
Variation of (a) maximum temperature and (b) maximum temperature difference with charge rates for four cooling configurations at a fixed flow rate of 4 L/min.
Variation of (a) maximum temperature and (b) maximum temperature difference with discharge rates for four cooling configurations at a fixed flow rate of 4 L/min.
Heat transfer coefficient during 4C discharging for all four cooling configurations at varying flow rates of (a) 2 L/min, (b) 3 L/min, and (c) 4 L/min.
Effect of fluid height ratio (x) on (a) maximum temperature and (b) maximum temperature difference at various charge rates, under a constant flow rate of 4 L/min.
Effect of fluid height ratio (x) on (a) maximum temperature and (b) maximum temperature difference at various discharge rates, under a constant flow rate of 4 L/min.
(a) Maximum temperature and (b) temperature difference of M9 during 4C discharging for different flow rates across four cooling configurations.
Technical specifications of an 18,650 battery cell.
Technical specifications of a 9S7P battery pack.
Dielectric fluid properties.
Average Heat Transfer Coefficients at 4C discharging.
Dielectric fluid volume and mass corresponding to different immersion heights for flow and spray cooling modes.
Pressure drop (kPa) for FIC and SIC configurations at different flow rates.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lithium-ion batteries have become the backbone of electrical vehicles, and managing their thermal behavior under high charge–discharge rates is increasingly important. This study introduces a novel spray-based immersion cooling method and experimentally compares its three partial submersion configurations against a conventional fully submerged flow-based immersion cooling system, using a 9S7P battery pack. The battery is operated under demanding charge and discharge rates ranging from 1C to 4C, while coolant flow rates are varied between 2 and 4 L/min. The results indicate that spray-based immersion cooling with minimal submersion achieves the lowest maximum cell temperatures and smallest temperature difference across the pack. This configuration reduces the dielectric liquid usage by up to 85 % compared to the flow-based approach. When subjected to high flow rates, this configuration delivers up to 19 % reduction in peak temperature, limiting it around 34 °C at 4C discharge rate. These results show that spray-based immersion cooling with low liquid volume and precise nozzle placement offers a highly promising direction for efficient, lightweight, and scalable battery thermal management system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Li-ion batteries are widely used in a large variety of scientific and engineering applications, especially in electrical vehicles (EVs), due to their high energy density and efficiency. Their usage in EVs is also crucial for reducing the transportation sector’s contribution to global carbon emissions [1]. However, charging and discharging these batteries at high C-rates can generate excessive heat which, if not dissipated, can significantly impact their performance and lifespan, and can lead to thermal runaway. Optimal performance can be obtained by maintaining the temperatures between 15 °C and 40 °C, and by maintaining the temperature difference between the battery cells and surroundings below 5 °C. This can be achieved by dissipating the heat generated within the battery pack during charge–discharge cycles.

A variety of battery thermal management systems (BTMSs) have been explored and analyzed using different cooling techniques to improve heat dissipation. Phase change materials (PCM)-based BTMSs improve battery temperature uniformity [2–4], but often fail to limit peak temperatures within the optimal range [2–8]. Hybrid PCM systems employing air or liquid cooling effectively reduce peak temperatures at discharge rates of up to 2C [9,10], however, they struggle to maintain thermal safety at higher charge/discharge rates [6,7]. Air-based cooling systems, on the other hand, are structurally simple and lightweight. They require careful optimization of airflow patterns, cell spacing etc. to achieve thermal uniformity and avoid excessive temperature gradients [11–13], but still are ineffective at high charge/discharge rates due to low heat transfer coefficient [14]. In contrast, the liquid-based BTMS has a higher heat transfer coefficient and thermal conductivity, making it one of the most preferred BTMSs in EVs [15].

Liquid-based BTMS can be categorized as either indirect or direct cooling. Indirect cooling involves circulating a coolant through cooling channels to transfer heat away from the battery pack, without direct contact between the coolant and the battery cells. Several recent studies have focused on developing indirect liquid-based BTMSs through innovative cooling channel designs and optimization strategies. Basu et al. [16] proposed a CFD-based model incorporating conduction and contact resistance effects, showing that low coolant flow rates can achieve sufficient thermal control with reduced energy consumption. A double-helix cooling structure was introduced by Dong et al. [17] that enhanced heat transfer while minimizing pressure drop through geometry optimization. Lyu et al. [18] demonstrated that combining liquid cooling with aerogel layers significantly delayed thermal runaway propagation, especially under reduced mass flow conditions. A numerical model highlighting the role of higher flow rates in lowering cell temperatures was suggested by Zhao et al. [19]. They also suggested channel geometry as a key design factor in their model. Zhou et al. [20] proposed a half-helical duct liquid cooling concept that effectively reduced maximum cell temperatures with minimal fluid usage. Sutheesh et al. [21] applied bio-inspired channel designs (fishbone and spiderweb) and found the fishbone layout superior for fluid distribution and thermal dissipation.

While these indirect liquid cooling outperforms conventional methods, its lack of direct contact with battery cells makes it less effective than direct liquid cooling, also known as immersion cooling, where the cells are immersed in a dielectric liquid. Tan et al. [22] in their research numerically studied direct liquid cooling of a fast-charging battery pack using hydrofluoroether. Their focus was to optimize channel height and cross-flow to improve temperature uniformity. In another numerical study, Kalkan et al. [23] identified optimal battery spacing and coolant flow to minimize pressure drop and temperature variance. The optimized setup achieved ΔT below 2.4 °C with a flow rate of 1.5 kg/s. Gao et al. [24] introduced a bionic immersion cooling system using fish-shaped flow guides to enhance coolant distribution. Fish-shaped holes reduced Tmax by 12.2 % and pump power by 42.1 %. Liu et al. [25] optimized immersion cooling using self-organizing manifold flow and checkerboard battery layout. Temperature difference across the module dropped by 43.8 %, with ΔT of 3.86 K.

Similarly, studies focusing on the type of dielectric liquid have highlighted its significant impact on thermal performance of the immersion-cooled battery systems. Jithin and Rajesh [26] compared various dielectric fluids for single-phase immersion cooling of lithium-ion batteries, including deionized water, mineral oil, and AmpCool AC-100. Their analysis revealed that deionized water provided the best cooling, limiting ΔT to 0.26 °C at 3C with 0.05 kg/s flow. Hemavathi et al. in their initial studies, evaluated a hydrostatic (passive) immersion cooling system using synthetic ester oil [27,28]. They further developed a forced flow immersion cooling system using the same dielectric liquid demonstrating significant improvement than static immersion and natural convection [29]. A maximum temperature drop of almost 30 % was recorded at higher Reynolds number. Chandrasekaran et al. [30] conducted experimental tests using AmpCool dielectric liquid in immersion cooling, showing significant improvement in thermal uniformity as compared to air cooling. A comparative study by Stayanarayana et al. [31] concluded that mineral oil provides better thermal uniformity compared to therminol oil.

A significant improvement in heat dissipation and thermal homogenization occurs when considering two-phase immersion cooling [32,33]. Li et al. in their multiple studies have used dielectric fluid with low boiling points to achieve two-phase cooling. When using SF33 as the fluid in their experimental evaluation, they found that peak temperatures were held under 35 °C during charging [34] while a reduction in Tmax by 12 °C was obtained during discharging [35]. A similar reduction of Tmax by 10.7 °C was observed by Khan et al. [36] in their experimental validation of SF33 immersion cooling. When using FS49 as the dielectric fluid, Li et al. experienced vast improvements in both temperature reduction and uniformity when compared to air cooling [37]. They also introduced a reciprocating immersion cooling system to improve idle-phase cooling [38]. Al-Zareer et al. developed two models using propane [39] and R134a [40] as the working fluids, highlighting boiling advantages in thermal regulation. They recorded peak temperature reductions exceeding 10 °C compared to non-boiling setups. Other noticeable factors studied in their research were immersion height and battery spacing [41].

Another method used by researchers to increase convection is the inclusion of jet impingement with immersion cooling. Song et al.’s CFD optimization showed six 3 mm jet pipes with 2:1 flow split offered superior cooling [42]. Their design cut Tmax from 39.1 °C to 28.8 °C and ΔT to 3.8 K. Trimbake et al.’s jet impingement model outperformed static immersion, especially at high C-rates [43]. At 2C charge and 3C discharge, mineral oil nearly maintains uniform skin temperature among cells. Zha et al. [44] compared drop cooling and full immersion methods using a multi-hole outlet design for coolant distribution. They found drop contact offered better thermal uniformity and lower temperature gradients than immersion contact system. A numerical study by Solai et al. [45] demonstrate a novel jet-grid system combining impingement with fluid film for 9 Samsung 40 T cells using Opteon SF10. Simulations confirm effectiveness of the grid technique in keeping battery temperature uniform and below hazardous thresholds.

Some researchers have introduced hybrid cooling system combining with spray in their studies. Dhuchakallaya and Saechan [46,47] combined hydrofluoroether (HFE) spray and forced air for a 40-cell cylindrical battery module. The study found that placing 20 g/s HFE nozzles between rows 1 and 2 reduced Tmax by 5.9 °C and ΔT by 4.0 °C over air cooling alone. Lei et al. [48], on the other hand, proposed a compact BTMS using heat pipes and intermittent spray cooling for LiFePO4 batteries under 1.92C discharge. Compared to natural and immersion cooling, the system lowered Tmax from 73.5 °C to 44.3 °C and reduced ΔT to 2.7 °C. A reciprocating mist and heat pipe BTMS was developed by Jiang et al. [49] avoiding direct battery wetting, and reducing Tmax to approximately 40.1 °C and ΔT to 3.41 °C. Later, they introduced a bidirectional mist system with hydrophilic-treated battery surfaces for improved pack cooling under 5C conditions [50]. Their experiments showed a 23 °C drop in peak temperature and >10 % improvement in thermal uniformity compared to non-treated surfaces. Majority of other studies have also primarily focused on hybrid spray cooling systems combining with airflow [51–55] or applied spray cooling primarily for mitigating thermal runaway events [56–64]. In contrast, most immersion cooling research has focused either on flow-based immersion, which demands large fluid volumes, or on two-phase immersion, which increases system complexity due to additional components. To address these limitations, the present work proposes a spray-based immersion cooling strategy that directly regulates the surface temperature of battery cells. Compared to jet nozzles, spray nozzles provide more uniform surface coverage across the battery pack, thereby enhancing cooling efficiency while reducing fluid usage.

In this study, an experimental comparison between flow-based and a proposed spray-based immersion cooling technique for the thermal management of Li-ion battery packs is presented. This is, to our knowledge, one of the first comparative studies that systematically evaluates spray immersion cooling versus conventional flow immersion cooling under identical conditions. The battery pack, operated under high charge and discharge rates, is tested under four different cooling cases including one utilizing the conventional flow-based immersion cooling (FIC) while the other three employing spray-based immersion cooling (SIC). In each of these cases, the dielectric fluid is either circulated or sprayed at different flow rates to study the impact of the proposed method on thermal performance. Key performance metrics, including the maximum cell temperature and the temperature gradient across the battery pack, are measured and compared. The amount of the dielectric fluid used in the experiment is also compared, as reducing the fluid usage offers benefits in terms of system weight and material cost, particularly important in large-scale applications such as EVs. The purpose of this comparative study is to
•
highlight the potential of SIC in maintaining battery temperatures within safe operating limits
•
investigate the influence of immersion depth on temperature uniformity within the battery pack, and maximum cell temperature during battery operation.
•
evaluate the potential of reduced-fluid spray cooling as an efficient and lightweight thermal management strategy for EV battery packs.
The discussion concludes with key takeaways from the experiments and a reflection on areas where further research could help improve the design and application of immersion cooling methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Battery thermal management design and experimental setup
ce:section.ce:para: The prerequisite for designing an effective battery thermal management system is the design of the battery itself. This section presents the details of the manufactured battery pack, including the type of cells used and their configuration. It also describes the housing design and the experimental setup developed to comparatively study the different immersion cooling cases.

A custom battery pack is assembled using Samsung INR18650-25R lithium-ion cells based on lithium cobalt oxide (LiCoO2), known for their high energy density and stable performance. The pack configuration consists of nine cells connected in series and seven such strings connected in parallel, forming a 9S7P layout as shown in Fig. 1 . 0.2 T 8 mm nickel strips were spot-welded to the cell terminals to ensure reliable electrical connections. Each individual 18650 cell has a cylindrical shape with dimensions of approximately 18 mm in diameter and 65 mm in height. The nominal voltage of a single cell is 3.6 V with a rated capacity of 2.5 Ah. When combined in the 9S7P configuration, the pack yields an overall nominal voltage of 32.4 V and a rated capacity of 17.5 Ah. Maximum charge voltage and cut-off voltage limits of the pack correspondingly scale to 37.8 V and 22.5 V, respectively. A summary of the key specifications for the individual cells and the complete battery pack is provided in Tables 1 and 2 , respectively.

Fig. 1 illustrates the configuration of the battery pack in a 9S7P arrangement. In this configuration, nine cells are connected in series to increase the overall voltage, while seven cells are connected in parallel to boost the pack's total capacity. The cylindrical cells are arranged with an inter-cell spacing of approximately 2 mm to allow dielectric liquid flow and accommodate thermal expansion. The figure also highlights the placement of T-type thermocouples, which are used to monitor the surface temperature of selected cells during operation. A total of 21 thermocouples are strategically distributed throughout the battery pack. Of these, 15 are mounted on the top layer with five each in the left (L), middle (M), and right (R) rows, as labeled in Fig. 2 . The remaining six thermocouples are attached to the center and bottom surfaces of three cells located in the middle row. This thermocouple distribution enables the evaluation of maximum temperature, intra-cell temperature variation, and the overall temperature gradient across the battery pack.

The thermocouple placement on the top surface of the battery pack is further illustrated in detail in Fig. 2. As shown, 15 thermocouples are installed across the top layer of the pack, distributed equally across three rows designated as L (Left), M (Middle), and R (Right). Within each row, thermocouples are connected to every alternate cell (e.g., L1, L3, L5, L7, L9), ensuring spatial coverage without redundancy. This layout helps in capturing localized temperature variations across the length of the battery pack. To maintain clarity throughout the study, a consistent cell nomenclature is introduced. Each cell is identified using a combination of its row (L, M, or R), its column number, and the thermocouple’s location. For example, M5B refers to a cell in the middle row and fifth column, with a thermocouple attached to its bottom. Similarly, T and C are used to indicate top and center positions of thermocouple attachment, respectively.

In addition to the thermocouple arrangement, Fig. 2 also provides a schematic of the experimental housing used for immersion cooling tests. It shows the battery pack positioned within the housing, as well as the designated fluid inlets and outlets for both cooling modes. In FIC, the dielectric liquid enters from the left side, passes through the battery pack, and exits to the right. In SIC, the liquid is sprayed via six nozzles located above the pack and is sprayed directly onto the top surface of the cells. The liquid then drains through the outlet on the right side, similar to the flow arrangement.

Fig. 3 presents the complete experimental setup developed for the immersion cooling study of the Li-ion battery pack. At the core of the setup is the KIKUSUI PFX2532 charge/discharge control system, which governs the battery pack's operation. This control system integrates a regulated DC power supply (PAT40-200T), a main electronic load (PLZ1205W), and two booster modules (PLZ2405WB). Together, they enable a maximum charging power of 8000 W and a discharge capacity of 6000 W, with the system’s overvoltage protection threshold set at 40 V by default. The battery cycling process is managed via a dedicated software application installed on a separate PC. This software allows the user to configure test parameters, execute charge–discharge cycles, and analyze results through a user-friendly interface. For clarity, Fig. 3 illustrates the spray immersion cooling (SIC) configuration, in which the inlet is located at the top of the left-side wall and diverged into six spray nozzles above the battery pack. In the flow immersion cooling (FIC) configuration, the inlet is instead positioned at the center of the left-side wall of the housing.

A VTI Instruments EX1032A Data Acquisition (DAQ) System is incorporated to accurately monitor thermal and pressure data during the experiments. Its 32-channel precision thermocouple module records temperature readings from thermocouples, while its 16-channel voltage measurement module captures output from a differential pressure transducer (Omega PX409), which measures the differential pressure between the inlet and outlet of the battery housing. LabVIEW is employed for real-time data visualization and monitoring of both temperature and pressure readings, ensuring precise control over the system's thermal dynamics.

The dielectric liquid used is 3 M’s Novec 7200, a specially engineered fluid known for its electrical insulation properties and environmental safety. It has a boiling point of 76 °C and a density of 1.43 g/cm3. Additional physical and environmental properties of the fluid are listed in Table 3 . Dielectric liquid circulation is driven by a magnetic turbine pump to maintain a steady, high flow rate through the closed-loop system. Right after the pump, a Coriolis mass flowmeter is installed to continuously measure the mass flow rate. The flowmeter requires a constant DC voltage supply and outputs both analog and pulse signals for precise tracking. Fluid’s temperature is regulated through a plate-type heat exchanger, which is thermally coupled with a bath circulator. The bath circulator maintains the distilled water temperature at 25 °C, thereby ensuring the dielectric liquid enters the battery pack at the same stable temperature.

The dielectric liquid enters the battery housing through a single inlet, which varies depending on the cooling approach considered, as illustrated in Fig. 4 . In the case of FIC (Case I), the inlet is a circular port on the left-side wall, allowing the battery pack to be fully submerged in the circulating dielectric liquid. This ensures convective heat transfer around the entire cell surface. In contrast, for the SIC cases (Cases II, III, and IV), the inlet is configured as six spray nozzles mounted above the battery pack. Here, only the lower portion of the battery pack is immersed in the dielectric liquid, while the upper portion is cooled by sprays delivered through nozzles with a wide spray angle of 120°, positioned 2 cm above the battery pack. This placement is carefully optimized to ensure uniform liquid distribution over all cells.

The primary variable distinguishing these SIC cases is the submersion height (h) of the battery pack, expressed as a fraction of the total cell height (H) as interpreted in Fig. 1. Accordingly, Case II corresponds to a submersion height of 0.5H, Case III to 0.3H, and Case IV to 0.1H. This variation allows for evaluating the thermal performance of partial submersion under otherwise identical operating conditions.

The immersion height was regulated by initially circulating the dielectric fluid through the entire closed-loop system, including the pump, heat exchanger, and flowmeter, to ensure stable operating conditions. After flow stabilization, the static fluid level within the battery housing was carefully adjusted to achieve the desired immersion depths (0.1H, 0.3H, or 0.5H), which were verified using calibrated markings on the transparent housing wall. During the experiments, the system operated under steady-state mass balance, ensuring that the total liquid volume and the immersion height remained constant throughout each test.

Battery is subjected to charge and discharge cycles at C-rates ranging from 1C to 4C. These high-power operating conditions are selected to simulate practical scenarios where significant heat is generated. The resulting thermal behavior is managed using the immersion cooling configurations described earlier. The dielectric liquid is circulated at three distinct flow rates (2, 3, and 4 L per minute) in order to assess the influence of flow rate on cooling effectiveness. This experimental plan is designed to examine the capability of each cooling method to control battery temperature during demanding charge/discharge operations.

To ensure the reliability of the collected data, the key measurement instruments used throughout the experiments are selected based on their accuracy and traceability. Mass flow rate, measured using a Coriolis flowmeter, has an accuracy of ±0.1 % of the observed value. Pressure drop across the cooling channel, recorded using a differential pressure transducer, offers a measurement accuracy of ±0.08 % of the best straight line (BSL). Temperature data are acquired through thermocouples with an uncertainty of ±0.5 °C. Building on these bounds, the averaged uncertainty in the calculated heat transfer coefficient (HTC) was estimated to be within ±14 %. These uncertainty bounds form an important foundation for the analysis presented in the next section, helping to contextualize both the absolute values and comparative trends in thermal performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature evolution under FIC and SIC(0.1H)
ce:section.ce:para: The two subplots in Fig. 5 display a comparison of temperature profiles of three cells M1, M5, and M9 during 4C charging under two distinct cooling configurations, case I (FIC) and case IV (SIC with 0.1H), operating at 4 L/min. It is immediately apparent that the FIC and SIC result in significantly different thermal behaviors across the selected cells. In the FIC plot, the temperature of the battery cells steadily increases, with M9B, the bottom of the M9 cell, experiencing the highest peak temperature, reaching approximately 36.3 °C. The bottom of the M5 cell follows similar trends but stays slightly cooler. In contrast, M1 remains comparatively cooler, with temperatures stabilizing in the range of 26–29 °C, indicating more effective cooling due to its position directly at the inlet of the housing.

In contrast, the spray-cooled cells demonstrate significantly improved thermal uniformity. Not only are the peak temperatures lower across all measured points, including M9B, but the temperature rise is also much more controlled. For instance, the M9B peak under SIC barely touches 30 °C, which is a substantial reduction of about 6 °C compared to the same point under FIC. Moreover, the temperature curves under SIC are tighter and show less variation among the cells. In FIC, the maximum temperature difference recorded is approximately 10 °C whereas in SIC, the maximum temperature difference recorded is a little lower than 4 °C. This reflects not only better cooling performance but also a more even distribution of the dielectric fluid over the battery surface, which is one of the key advantages of spray-based methods.

Fig. 6 illustrates the two subplots depicting the temperature behavior of the same cells under similar two configurations as previously used (case I and case IV), during 4C discharging conditions operating at a similar flow rate of 4 L/min. The trends observed during charging are further reinforced here, but the thermal stress is more severe. Under FIC, the temperature of M9B spikes dramatically, peaking at 42 °C near the end of the discharge cycle. Significant heating is also seen in M5B with distinct temperatures visible across the vertical layers of both M5 and M9 cells. M1 again maintains relatively lower temperatures, suggesting that the location within the pack plays a substantial role in the thermal response when using FIC.

With SIC, however, the situation improves considerably. The temperature peaks of all three cells are significantly suppressed, with M9B topping out just under 34 °C, a reduction of more than 8 °C compared to FIC. Similarly, M5B shows a temperature drop of approximately 9 °C, highlighting the enhanced cooling effectiveness of spray configuration. Not only are the absolute values lower, but the entire discharge temperature curve is flatter and smoother, which is indicative of better thermal control throughout the cycle. The disparity between the top, center, and bottom temperature points within the same cell and between different cells within the battery pack is also much narrower, further validating the improved uniformity offered by the spray method.

What is especially noteworthy here is that the central and bottom regions of the two hottest cells (M9 and M5) benefit the most from 0.1H SIC configuration. This suggests that SIC has better reach and coverage in areas where FIC may suffer from stagnation or poor local convection due to geometric constraints. It also hints at the ability of SIC to actively target hot spots, a desirable trait for maintaining battery performance and safety. Further discussion on this is included later in this section where different SIC cases are compared with one another. Another point worth highlighting is the gradient of individual temperatures. Under FIC, the temperature increases more steeply and continues to climb until it reaches its peak at the center of charge cycle or near the end of the discharge cycle. In contrast, the SIC method appears to have smaller rise in the temperature earlier, effectively preventing high gradients during the stages of the cycles when batteries are typically more vulnerable to excessive heat buildup.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative thermal performance of cooling configurations
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 illustrates the maximum temperature profiles during 4C charging for four cases presented in Fig. 4, evaluated at three different flow rates (2, 3, and 4 L/min). As the flow rate increases, a consistent reduction in the peak temperature is observed across all four cases with SIC consistently outperforming FIC. Notably, SIC with 0.1H submersion always yields the lowest maximum temperatures. At 2 L/min, the peak temperatures for FIC and 0.5H SIC reach approximately 37 °C, while the 0.3H spray case remains slightly lower. However, the SIC (0.1H) configuration shows a substantial temperature reduction, with a maximum around 32 °C. This pattern continues for higher flow rates: at 3 L/min, FIC peaks at 37 °C, SIC (0.5H and 0.3H) show moderate improvement, and SIC (0.1H) maintains a peak temperature around 30.5 °C. The trend is further enhanced at 4 L/min, where overall temperatures drop slightly, but the comparative effectiveness of SIC (0.1H) becomes even more evident, reaching a maximum temperature of around 30 °C. FIC and SIC (0.5H and 0.3H) result in higher temperature profiles due to lower convective heat transfer near the cell’s bottom surface. In contrast, SIC with 0.1H offers more localized and effective surface cooling, leading to lower cell temperatures.

Fig. 8 , similarly, demonstrates the maximum temperature profiles during 4C discharging, evaluated for the same four cases and three flow rates. The impact of flow rate during discharging mirrors that during charging, with higher flow rates leading to reduced maximum temperatures. However, due to higher heat generation during discharging, especially in later stages of DOD (Depth of Discharge), the temperature reduction offered by increasing flow rate is less pronounced for FIC and spray (0.5H) compared to the charging case. At 2 L/min, the flow-cooled configuration reaches temperatures slightly above 42 °C, indicating insufficient heat dissipation. SIC provides substantial improvement with the 0.1H configuration limiting peak temperature to below 38 °C. Increasing the flow rate to 3 L/min results in a little drop in peak temperature across all configurations, but spray (0.1H) remains the most effective, maintaining temperature below 34.5 °C. At 4 L/min, all configurations exhibit further reduction in maximum temperature, with spray (0.1H) effectively capping the temperature at approximately 34 °C. Similar to charging, FIC and SIC (0.3H and 0.5H) setups consistently show the higher temperature curves, whereas SIC with 0.1H configuration demonstrate significantly better cooling. This is more evident at the end of DOD, where heat generation spikes and SIC proves crucial.

The temperature curves shown in Figs. 7 and 8 give a clear picture of how a Li-ion battery heats up differently during charging and discharging. These differences come down to how voltage and capacity behave in each case. During charging, the temperature rises rapidly at first, peaks around 50–60 % SOC, and then decreases as SOC approaches 100 %. The rise in temperature occurs when the battery is charged at a constant current. During this phase, the voltage rises quickly and reaches the maximum set voltage i.e. 4.2 V per cell (see Table 1 for details) well before the battery is fully charged. The rapid initial voltage rise is due to polarization and overvoltage effects, which are more pronounced during charging, especially at higher currents. At this maximum voltage, the temperature also reaches its maximum after which the charger switches to constant voltage mode (see Fig. 9 a). The voltage is held steady, and the current gradually decreases as the battery continues to accept charge. This stage is responsible for filling up the remaining capacity. Kantharaj et al. [65] and Hemavathi et al. [27] explain this heat generation using the Bernardi [66] equation:

At higher charging current, the reversible heat is relatively small and can be neglected, simplifying the expression to:

where I denotes current and R represents the internal resistance. This shows that heat generation is proportional to the square of the current. Therefore, in the constant voltage phase, the decline in current results in reduced heat generation, which is reflected in the downward trend of the temperature curve after the peak in Fig. 7.

In contrast, during discharging, the temperature increases more steadily and typically, reaches maximum near the end of the discharge cycle. Unlike charging, the current remains relatively constant over most of the discharge period, and the battery voltage decreases more gradually and linearly with capacity (see Fig. 9b). Because the current does not decrease significantly until near the end of the cycle, heat generation continues throughout the discharge, and the internal resistance may increase due to the changes in the battery’s electrochemical environment, such as electrolyte depletion, at the end of discharge cycle [67]. This contributes to increased Joule heating effect and thus greater heat generation toward the end of discharge. As a result, the temperature continues to rise, reaching its maximum close to full discharge, as shown in Fig. 8.

Fig. 10 highlights the coldest and hottest regions that develop across the battery due to the dielectric liquid flow distribution. As mentioned earlier, in FIC, the coldest region appears along the mid-height of cell M1, which is directly exposed to the inward flow of the dielectric liquid at the upstream entry. This ensures strong convective cooling in that localized region. In SIC (all 3 cases), however, the coldest regions consistently develop on the top surfaces of multiple cells, as the impinging spray provides the highest cooling effectiveness at the point of impact.

The hottest region, common to both configurations, occurs near the bottom side of M9, located at the downstream end of the pack. This region experiences reduced coolant accessibility, weaker flow circulation, and limited convective exchange, all of which hinder heat removal. Although M5 also shows signs of higher local heating at its bottom surface, M9 is more severely affected due to its downstream placement, which makes it more prone to reaching higher temperatures. Moreover, even when the flow rate is increased by 100 %, the cooling effect at these bottom regions particularly around cells M9, M5, and M1 remains minimally affected. This is because the local flow accessibility and convective exchange at the lower boundaries are inherently restricted, preventing proportional improvements in peak temperature reduction despite the higher bulk flow.

Figs. 11 and 12
demonstrate the temperature difference (ΔT) between these hottest and coldest points in a battery pack. Similar to Figs. 7 and 8, each figure shows the comparison for the four cases across three flow rates under 4C charging and discharging. During charging, the ΔT increases initially with SOC and peaks around 50–60 % SOC for all cases. At 2 L/min, the SIC at 0.1H performs the best, limiting the temperature difference below 6 °C throughout the cycle. As the immersion height increases to 0.3H and 0.5H, the effectiveness decreases, with ΔT peaking around and beyond 10 °C, respectively. FIC shows better performance compared to SIC (0.3H and 0.5H), with ΔT reaching around 9 °C (see Figs. 15 and 16 for further details).

With 3 L/min, all SIC cases show improved uniformity. The 0.1H SIC case continues to outperform, keeping ΔT consistently under 5 °C, while 0.3H and 0.5H maintain the difference just below 10 °C. FIC shows unusual shift with the difference increasing from 9 °C to 10 °C. The reason for this unusual shift is explained later in the text. At 4 L/min, all SIC cases benefit from the increased flow rate, with reduced peak temperature differences. SIC at 0.1H now maintains a gradient lower than 4 °C, and even 0.3H performs significantly better with 7.5 °C difference than the corresponding case at 2 L/min. FIC still remains less effective than SIC with maintaining the same difference as in 3 L/min.

Discharging behavior, shown in Fig. 12, reveals higher temperature difference compared to charging due to the generally higher internal heat generation during discharging at 4C. The ΔT increases progressively with DOD, peaking near the end of the cycle (∼95 % DOD). At 2 L/min, FIC reaches a peak ΔT of approximately 13.5 °C, while SIC at 0.5H and 0.3H surpass this with a peak ΔT of 16.5 °C and 15.5 °C, respectively, indicating that a higher immersion height is less effective at low flow rate. However, SIC at 0.1H significantly suppresses the ΔT, maintaining it below 11 °C. At 3 L/min, the ΔT reduces for all configurations except for the FIC similar to that during charging. FIC peaks around 14 °C increasing previously from 13.5 °C, whereas spray at 0.3H is around 14 °C, and 0.1H keeps ΔT under 8 °C. SIC at 0.5H still gives the maximum with 15 °C despite reducing by a margin of 1.5 °C from a previous high of 16.5 °C. At 4 L/min, SIC at 0.1H shows the best performance with maximum ΔT around 7 °C. FIC increases further to a high of almost 15 °C while spray at 0.5H maintains the difference at 15 °C. Spray at 0.3H shows ΔT values around 13 °C. This shows that higher flow rates and spray with lower immersion heights improve cooling uniformity, especially under critical discharging conditions.

In the case of FIC, ΔT increases during both charging and discharging with the increase in flow rate. This is because the convective heat transfer at the M1C point increases with the increase in flow rate, while the bottom surface of the battery pack experiences, relatively, no increase in convection, resulting in an increased temperature difference across the pack. The little to no change in maximum temperature for FIC can be seen in Fig. 8 which highlights that the increased temperature difference is purely due to decrease in temperature at the M1C point. On the contrary, in SIC, the reduction in ΔT with reducing immersion heights highlights the effect of enhanced dielectric liquid circulation at the bottom surface. This comparison is further highlighted later in this section.

Figs. 13 and 14
compare the response of different cooling strategies to increasing electrical load during charging and discharging at a flow rate of 4 L/min. The extent of maximum temperature rises and larger temperature differences vary noticeably among different configurations. Starting with the charging results shown in Fig. 13, at a low charge rate (1C), the maximum temperature recorded with FIC is around 26.5 °C, while SIC at 0.1H achieves a slightly lower value of 25.8 °C. As the charge rate increases to 4C, the maximum temperature for FIC rises sharply to about 36.5 °C, compared to 35.2 °C for spray (0.5H), 33.8 °C for spray (0.3H), and 30.2 °C for spray (0.1H). A similar trend is observed in the temperature difference subplot. At 1C, ΔT is minimal for all configurations roughly 1.2 °C for flow and less than 0.5 °C for spray (0.1H). However, at 4C, the difference grows significantly. FIC reaches a ΔT of 10.2 °C, while spray at 0.5H and 0.3H manage to restrict it to around 9.2 °C and 7.5 °C, respectively. Spray at 0.1H again performs best, limiting the difference to only 3.9 °C.

A similar but more pronounced thermal response is observed when looking at the discharging results in Fig. 14. At 1C, FIC shows a maximum temperature of approximately 28 °C, whereas spray (0.1H) maintains it at 26.6 °C. At 4C, the difference becomes more noticeable with FIC reaching a peak of around 42 °C, spray (0.5H) recording 41.5 °C, spray (0.3H) peaking at 39.4 °C, and spray (0.1H) containing it to 34 °C.

In terms of temperature uniformity during discharging, the internal temperature difference reaches 14.8 °C for SIC (0.5H) at 4C highlighting the thermal stress on the system while FIC and spray at 0.3H show better control with 14.8 °C and 13 °C, respectively. Spray (0.1H) again performs significantly better, limiting the difference to about 7 °C even under the highest load.

Fig. 15 illustrates the variation of heat transfer coefficient with DOD at flow rates of 2 L/min, 3 L/min, and 4 L/min, respectively. Similarly, Table 4 presents the average HTC values for each configuration at different flow rates. The data confirm that SIC with shallow submersion (0.1H) is consistently superior, providing up to 100 % improvement in HTC over FIC. Across all cases, the SIC configuration with 0.1H submersion consistently demonstrated the highest HTC. At 2 L/min, HTC values exceeded 320 W/m2·K, increasing further to in excess of 450 W/m2·K at 4 L/min. This superior performance is attributed to the direct impingement of spray droplets on the heated cell surfaces, enhancing convective mixing and reducing thermal resistance.

By contrast, the FIC case showed significantly lower HTC values. At 2 L/min, the average HTC was 169.6 W/m2·K, which improved to 247.9 W/m2·K at 3 L/min. However, a subsequent decrease to 208.9 W/m2·K was observed at 4 L/min (Table 4). This non-monotonic behavior suggests that simply increasing the bulk flow alone does not necessarily translate into higher effective heat transfer. The most likely reason is the formation of larger bypass flow paths at higher flow rates, where the fluid past the side and upper regions of the pack without sufficiently interacting with the surfaces. This reduces effective convective exchange despite increased total flow.

Intermediate SIC depths (0.3H and 0.5H) yielded moderate HTC values in the range of 240–300 W/m2·K. These results indicate that while partial submersion improves cooling compared to FIC, the efficiency decreases with increasing immersion depth due to reduced spray impingement effectiveness at the bottom of the pack.

Fig. 16 presents a comprehensive analysis of how the immersion fluid height affects both the maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference within a lithium-ion battery pack under various charging rates (1C, 2C, 3C, and 4C) at a fixed dielectric liquid flow rate of 4 L/min. The two subplots in the figure show the variation of maximum temperature and maximum temperature difference as a function of fluid height ratio (x = h/H).

It is evident that the maximum temperature decreases with decreasing fluid height for all charge rates. This is a direct indication that the cooling efficiency is enhanced with lesser immersion of the battery in the dielectric fluid. At a fluid height ratio of 0.5, the highest maximum temperatures are observed across all C-rates. For example, at 4C, the maximum temperature is approximately 35.2 °C, whereas it drops significantly to around 30.2 °C at a fluid height of 0.1H. Similarly, for 3C, 2C, and 1C, a consistent temperature reduction is observed as immersion decreases. The steepest decline in temperature occurs between h = 0.3H and h = 0.1H, particularly for 4C, suggesting that even small decreases in fluid coverage can have a significant thermal impact under high-rate charging. This highlights the importance of fluid-submersion coverage in managing peak temperatures, especially during fast charging cases where heat generation is considerably high. Similar to the maximum temperature trend, ΔT decreases as fluid height decreases. However, this trend is counterintuitive at first glance where it could be expected that partial immersion leads to poorer cooling and hence greater thermal difference. Yet, the data shows that the largest ΔT occurs at h = 0.5H, reaching as high as 9 °C at 4C. As h reduces to 0.1H, ΔT drops to approximately 3.8 °C.

Similar to Fig. 16, Fig. 17 shows the influence of immersion fluid height on both the maximum temperature and the maximum temperature difference within the battery pack during discharging at different rates but at constant flow rate of 4 L/min. In the maximum temperature subplot (Fig. 17a), a clear trend emerges across all discharge rates showing a decrease in temperature with a reduction in fluid height ratio. This also indicates that a lesser proportion of the battery being immersed in the dielectric liquid leads to better heat dissipation and therefore lower peak temperatures. The effect is most pronounced at the highest discharge rate of 4C, where the maximum temperature drops sharply from around 41.6 °C at h = 0.5H to approximately 34 °C at h = 0.1H.

At lower discharge rates (1C and 2C), although the temperature reduction with decreasing fluid height is less dramatic, the trend remains consistent. For instance, at 1C, the maximum temperature varies modestly from 27.5 °C at h = 0.5H to 26.5 °C at h = 0.1H, indicating that the thermal load is significantly lower and more easily managed at slower discharge rates. This behavior underscores the importance of immersion depth in controlling thermal performance during discharging. High discharge rates generate more heat as a result of due to greater ohmic heating within the cells, requiring more aggressive cooling to maintain safe operating temperatures.

The maximum temperature difference graph in Fig. 17b serves as an indicator of thermal non-uniformity showing the maximum temperature difference increases with increasing fluid height. At 4C, for instance, the ΔT is highest at 15 °C for h = 0.5H and falls sharply to around 6.5 °C for h = 0.1H. For lower discharge rates like 1C and 2C, both the maximum temperature and the temperature difference remain within safe and manageable levels. The maximum temperature difference at 1C ranges from about 2.5 °C at h = 0.5H to 1.5 °C at h = 0.1H, suggesting that minimal immersion is sufficient to maintain thermal uniformity under low-load conditions.

The phenomenon in decreasing maximum temperature at a constant charge/discharge rate and a constant flow rate can be attributed to the fluid velocity at the bottom of the battery pack. Decreasing the immersion height while keeping the flow rate constant increases the fluid velocity resulting in increased convective heat transfer at the bottom surface, hence, reducing the maximum temperature. Similarly, the phenomenon in temperature difference can be attributed to the vertical thermal gradient introduced by partial immersion. At higher immersion levels, only part of the battery (50 % in 0.5H and 70 % in 0.3H) is under direct spray influence with high convection, leading to cooler regions at the top and warmer regions at the bottom. This creates a strong vertical gradient and increases the temperature difference. In contrast, at lower immersion levels (h = 0.1H), the thermal field is more uniform since 90 % of the battery is under spray influence, resulting in a smaller ΔT.

Table 5 highlights the comparative overview of the dielectric fluid volume and mass required inside the housing for the cases considered. For each method, the table lists the corresponding cell immersion height h, fluid volume V, and fluid mass m used in the experiment. In FIC, the battery is entirely submerged in the dielectric fluid, requiring a total fluid volume of 2.34 L and a fluid mass of 3.35 kg. This is the maximum amount of fluid needed across all methods and serves as a baseline for evaluating efficiency. As the immersion level decreases through partial SIC, the required volume and mass of fluid decrease significantly. At a 50 % SIC (h = 0.5H), only 1.59 L of fluid is needed, reducing the fluid mass to 2.27 kg, a substantial decrease of nearly 32 % in mass compared to full immersion. The reduction is even more dramatic at 0.3H and 0.1H immersion levels, where the fluid mass drops to 1.39 kg and 0.509 kg, respectively, marking an approximate 85 % reduction at the lowest immersion level.

Table 6 presents the pressure drop measurement across the dielectric fluid flow system for the cases studied. The data clearly shows that the pressure drop in FIC is significantly lower than in all spray configurations. For example, at 2 L/min, the pressure drop in FIC is only 0.535 kPa, whereas the spray configurations show values around 12 kPa, which is over 20 times higher. This trend persists and becomes more pronounced at higher flow rates. At 4 L/min, the pressure drop in FIC is 2.25 kPa, while the spray systems exhibit a little higher values: 44.5–55 kPa, depending on the immersion height.

Interestingly, spray at 0.1H shows a slightly higher pressure drop (55 kPa) than the other spray cases at 4 L/min, likely due to greater fluid resistance or turbulence. However, beyond 2 L/min, the pressure drop values for spray at 0.3H and 0.5H are nearly identical, suggesting that the flow dynamics reach a similar resistance profile as the spray spreads and stabilizes over a larger area.

The results shown in Fig. 18 present a more comprehensive look into the thermal behavior of the single hottest cell (M9) during 4C discharging. The maximum temperature remains relatively high across all flow rates for FIC, ranging narrowly around 42–43 °C, indicating limited sensitivity to increased flow rate. SIC with 0.1H outperforms all others. As the submersion height is lowest, the dielectric fluid velocity at the bottom enhances heat removal significantly. At 4 L/min, the maximum temperature drops to around 34 °C, which is a notable improvement of almost 8–9 °C compared to flow immersion. Even at 2 L/min, the temperature remains well below that of any other configuration. As the submersion height is increased, thermal performance declines. At 0.3H, the temperature drops marginally from 42.5 °C to 39.5 °C with flow rate showing moderate improvement. However, at 0.5H, the performance is only slightly better than FIC, especially at lower flow rates.

The temperature difference within the cell in FIC (Fig. 18b) shows a moderate rise with from 7 °C at 2 L/min to 9.6 °C at 4 L/min increasing flow rate, further solidifying the claim that increasing flow rate does not necessarily improve thermal uniformity in FIC. In contrast, the SIC method with 0.1H exhibits a clear improvement in thermal performance with increased flow rate. It shows a drop in ΔT from 9.7 °C at 2 L/min to 6.3 °C at 4 L/min. SIC with 0.3H, however, performs less effectively than that with 0.1H, recording ΔT values from 14.6 °C for 2 L/min to 12.2 °C for 4 L/min. At 0.5H, SIC struggles more with high ΔT of minimum 14.2 °C at 4 L/min.

This data underscores that SIC at 0.1H height performs better than flow immersion, showing a lower maximum temperature and a lower ΔT especially at higher flow rates. However, SIC at 0.3H and 0.5H reveals a different trend despite higher flow rates. While maximum temperatures show slight improvements, the ΔT remains significantly elevated, suggesting these SIC cases with higher dielectric liquid submersion are less effective at managing intra-cell hotspots under the demanding 4C charging condition.

By employing targeted cooling strategies like SIC with minimal immersion heights, the system becomes lighter and more compact. Using less fluid also reduces the risk and complexity of fluid handling, leakage, and disposal. A lower fluid mass means less environmental impact, both during operation and at the end of the fluid’s lifecycle. Minimizing the quantity of fluid used supports design scalability and modularity. As battery packs become more compact and energy-dense, targeted and efficient cooling with small fluid volumes becomes increasingly essential for safe and sustainable operation.

This study presents the design and experimental evaluation of a Li-ion battery thermal management system employing flow-based immersion cooling (FIC) and spray-based immersion cooling (SIC) across varying submersion levels. A 9S7P battery pack was tested under rigorous charge/discharge conditions (1C–4C) and at flow rates ranging from 2 to 4 L/min, using key metrics such as maximum cell temperature, internal temperature difference (ΔT), temperature difference across battery pack, and pressure drop. Following conclusions are drawn from this experimental study:
1.
The spray immersion cooling (SIC) method demonstrated superior thermal management compared to conventional flow immersion cooling, achieving lower maximum battery surface temperatures and reduced temperature gradients. This highlights the strong potential of SIC for mitigating thermal risks in Li-ion battery systems.
2.
Reducing immersion height to 0.1H provided the best cooling performance, primarily due to increased fluid velocity at the bottom of the pack and improved uniformity of spray coverage, resulting in both lower maximum temperatures and smaller temperature differences.
3.
The study confirms that SIC offers not only effective thermal control but also practical applicability. It achieves up to 85 % reduction in dielectric fluid usage, thereby lowering system weight and material costs, while simultaneously decreasing temperature difference by 32 % and maximum cell temperature by 19 % compared to conventional immersion cooling.
4.
These improvements came with increased pressure drops, especially at higher flows, which could affect system efficiency and complexity. Overall, the findings suggest that spray cooling with optimized submersion height and fluid velocity can offer better thermal control and can emerge as a highly promising strategy for next generation battery systems.

Despite its benefits, SIC with 0.1H submersion experiences higher temperatures at the bottom of the cells under heavy loads. This limitation arises from restricted convection and spray reach at the lower boundaries. Therefore, future research should focus on improving bottom-side cooling through optimized nozzle design, spray angles, advanced flow routing, or hybrid strategies without increasing system complexity or pressure drop, ultimately achieving more balanced and efficient thermal management throughout the entire battery structure.

The present study was limited to short-term thermal characterization using surface-mounted thermocouples and did not include electrochemical degradation tests such as RPT or DCIR. Furthermore, scalability factors such as pumping power, pressure drop, and long-term dielectric fluid stability were not fully addressed. Future work will include coupled thermal–electrochemical–CFD modeling, long-term cycling with electrochemical performance checks, and techno-economic assessments of fluid usage and power input, providing a pathway toward industrial-scale applicability.
